facts.text: Blauvelt’s name traces back to Dutch trader Pieter Blauwveld, whose coat of arms meant “blue‑field,” a nod to the blue and yellow shields on his ships.
The first Blauvelt in America was a peasant farmer who worked Kiliaen van Rensselaer's tobacco fields in 1638.
In 1909, New York State bought land in Blauvelt to build Camp Bluefields, a rifle range that trained the National Guard before World I.
The Blauvelt house, erected in 1832, remained in the family for nearly two centuries before the Historical Society of Rockland County took ownership in 1970.
The Historical Society preserves two early Rockland County homestead maps, one dating back to 1750, offering a glimpse into the area’s colonial past.
Johannes Isaac Blauvelt House, located at 453 N., is one of the historic homes highlighted by the Association of Blauvelt Descendants.
